markdown:
1. The central bank has **different** **tools** to **control** the money **creation** in the economy:
  1. **Open Market Operations**
  2. **Minimum Reserve Requirements**
  3. **Changes in Central Bank Minimum Lending Rate**
  4. **Quantitative Easing**

## Open Market Operations


Open market operations work with the **buying** and **selling** of government **bonds in the bond market.**

<definition term="Bond">Refers to a **signed certificate**, which **represents** a loan **given** by the **bond holder to the borrower** in exchange for **regular interest payments** and the **promise** to** repay the full amount** at a **set future date**.</definition>

<callout type="note">1. The **buyer** of the bond is the **lender**
2. The **seller** of the bond is the **borrower**</callout>

1. If the central bank wants to** lower the interest rates,** it can** increase the money supply** in the economy by** buying government bonds** from the commercial banks. 
  1. This will **increase the amount of money these commercial banks hold**, which **increases** the amount of **loans** they can give.
  2. This in turn, **increases the money supply** and leads to **decreasing interest rates**.
2. If the central bank wants to **increase** the **interest** **rates**, it should **decrease the money supply in the economy**, by **selling** government **bonds** to **commercial** **banks**.
  1. As commercial banks **pay for these bonds**, they will have **less money to give out as loans **which will **decrease the money supply in the economy and hence will lead to higher interest rates.**

<callout type="note">We will discuss in depth how central banks change **interest rates**, and influence the **supply** **and** **demand** **of** **money** in the next section. For now, just understand the differences between the **tools central bank** have to carry out **monetary policy**.</callout>

## Minimum Reserve Requirements


1. As we already discussed in the last section:
  1. If the** minimum reserve requirements are decreased** by the central banks:
      1. The **commercial** banks reserves **increase**, **increasing** their **lending ability**.
    2. The **supply of money will increase** as more money will be created.
  2. If the **minimum reserve requirements are increased** by the central banks:
      1. The **commercial** banks reserves **decrease**, **decreasing** their **lending ability**.
    2. The **supply of money will decrease** as less money will be created.

## Changes in the central bank minimum lending rate


1. Central banks can also **lend money** to **commercial** **banks**, for which they **charge** interest rate, called **minimum lending rate**.
  1. When the **central bank** wants to **increase** the **money** **supply**, it **lowers** the **minimum lending rate**.
      1. This **encourages** **commercial banks** to **borrow** **more** **money **as it is **more** **affordable**, hence **increasing** the **money** **supply**.
  2. When the **central bank** wants to **decrease** the **money** **supply**, it **raises** the **minimum lending rate**.
      1. This **discourages** **commercial banks** from **borrowing** **money **as it is** more expensive**, hence **decreasing** the **money** **supply**.

### Quantitative easing


<definition term="Quantitative Easing">A **monetary policy** where the **central bank** **purchases** **financial assets** from the **market** to **increase** the **money** **supply**.</definition>

1. Quantitative easing is a policy utilised when **interest rates** are **already** **very** **low** and **cannot** be **reduced** **further**.
2. This is where the **central bank** **buys** **financial assets** (like **bonds **and many other types) from the **market**, **injecting** **money** into the **economy**.
  1. Essentially, the **central bank buys quantities of assets** from commercial banks by **creating electronic reserves** for **commercial** **banks**.
  2. This **increases** commercial bank **reserves**, which **increases** the **money** **supply **and **lowers** **interest rates** as they can **lend** **more**.

<callout type="note">Quantitative easing can lead to **inflation **if not **managed properly**.</callout>
